name: Linux & Command Line
description: Shell essentials: permissions, processes, pipes, logs and networking basics.

questions.text: Which command changes a file's permissions?
options: chmod, which sets the read/write/execute bits
chgrp, which moves the file into another group
ls, which lists the files in the directory
chown, which transfers the file to a new owner
questions.explanation: chmod sets the read/write/execute bits; chown changes ownership and chgrp changes the group.

questions.text: In rwx permissions, what does the 'x' allow for a file?
options: Reading the contents of the file
Executing or running the file directly
Renaming the file to something else
Deleting the file from its directory
questions.explanation: x means execute for files (run it) and, for directories, permission to enter/traverse them.

options: Turns the rest of the line into a comment
Redirects the output into a named file
Feeds one command's output into the next
Runs the command as a background job
questions.explanation: Pipes chain commands, e.g. `cat log | grep ERROR` feeds cat's output into grep.

options: '>>' deletes the file before writing to it
'>' overwrites the file; '>>' appends to it
'>' appends to the file; '>>' overwrites it
Both simply overwrite the target file
questions.explanation: Use >> to add to a file without wiping it; > truncates and replaces the contents.

options: The two commands behave exactly the same way
kill -9 is the gentler of the two signals
kill restarts the process instead of ending it
Plain kill asks nicely; -9 forces a stop
questions.explanation: Prefer plain kill so the process can shut down cleanly; -9 is a last resort that skips cleanup.

questions.text: How do you follow a log file as it grows?
options: head file, which shows only the first lines
tail -f, which streams new lines as written
cat file, which prints it once and exits
less file, which opens it a single time
questions.explanation: tail -f streams new lines as they're written — the standard way to watch logs live.

options: Permanently deletes files without confirming
Reports how much disk space is being used
Runs a command with elevated root privileges
Searches a file for lines matching a pattern
questions.explanation: sudo executes a single command as root (or another user), used for privileged operations.

options: Terminate a running process by its name
Change the permissions on a group of files
Copy files from one directory to another
Search text for lines matching a pattern
questions.explanation: grep filters input to lines matching a pattern, e.g. finding errors in a log.

questions.text: Which shows currently running processes with live CPU/memory usage?
options: ls, a listing of files in the directory
grep, a search through lines of text
cd, which changes the current directory
top (or htop), a live view of processes
questions.explanation: top gives a live view of processes and resource use; ps gives a one-shot snapshot.

options: The directories the shell searches for programs
A single file listing your recent commands
The full path to your personal home directory
A permission bit controlling who may execute
questions.explanation: When you type a command, the shell looks through the directories in $PATH to find it.

options: df shows free space; du shows space used
Both report exactly the same disk figures
df shows used space; du shows what is free
df lists files; du counts words in them
questions.explanation: df answers 'how full is the disk?'; du answers 'what's taking up space here?'.

options: '/', the root of the whole filesystem
'~', a shortcut to your home directory
'./', meaning the current working directory
a letter naming the drive it lives on
questions.explanation: Absolute paths begin at / and are unambiguous; relative paths are interpreted from the current directory.

options: Opens the script in the default text editor
Makes the script executable so you can run it
Runs the script immediately as the root user
Permanently deletes the script from the disk
questions.explanation: It adds the execute bit so you can run the file directly, e.g. ./script.sh.

questions.text: The exit code of the last command is in $?. What does 0 mean?
options: That the command could not be found on PATH
Success; any non-zero value signals an error
That permission to run the command was denied
Failure; the command did not run correctly
questions.explanation: Convention: 0 means success, any non-zero value signals an error — scripts check this to branch.

questions.text: Which safely copies a file to a remote host over SSH?
options: mv, which renames or moves a local file
scp (or rsync), copying over the SSH connection
cp, which only copies on the local machine
cat, which prints a file to the screen
questions.explanation: scp uses the SSH connection to copy files between machines; rsync is similar with delta transfers.

questions.text: Files beginning with a dot (e.g. .bashrc) are…
options: Always executable programs or scripts
Reserved strictly for system use only
Hidden by default; ls -a reveals them
Broken files the system could not read
questions.explanation: Dotfiles are hidden from a normal ls; they usually hold configuration.

questions.text: less vs cat for viewing a large file?
options: less paginates and lets you scroll and search
cat pages through the file one screen at a time
less loads the whole file into an editor
They behave identically for large files
questions.explanation: For big files less is far more usable — you can page through and search without flooding the terminal.

options: Compresses files to save space on disk
Ends processes that use too much CPU
Edits text files from the command line
Runs commands automatically on a schedule
questions.explanation: cron executes jobs at defined times/intervals — the classic way to schedule recurring tasks.

options: stderr is the input typed in by the user
They are one combined stream, not two
stdout is normal output; stderr is for errors
stdout carries errors; stderr carries results
questions.explanation: Separating them lets you redirect errors independently, e.g. 2> errors.log.

options: Reboot the machine back to a clean state
Simply list the files under the root directory
Recursively force-delete everything — catastrophic
Do nothing because root is always protected
questions.explanation: It's the canonical dangerous command; -r recurses, -f forces, and / is everything. Handle with extreme care.

options: The size on disk of a given file
The permission bits set on a file
How much disk space a folder is using
The path of the program that would run
questions.explanation: which resolves a command name to the actual binary found on your $PATH.

questions.text: How do you run a long task in the background from the shell?
options: Append '&' to start it in the background
Press Ctrl-C to detach it from the shell
Type 'bg' before the command to launch it
Prefix it with sudo to free the terminal
questions.explanation: Appending & starts the job in the background; jobs, fg and bg manage it afterwards.

options: Compressing a file to take less space
Searching a file for a matching pattern
Setting the read/write/execute permission bits
Changing a file's owner and its group
questions.explanation: chown reassigns ownership; combine with chmod to control who can do what.

options: Opens the manual page documenting ls
Deletes the ls command from the system
Runs ls and saves the output to a file
Lists every manual page installed locally
questions.explanation: man shows built-in documentation for a command — the first place to look for options.

options: Both print the entire file from the start
head shows the first lines; tail the last
They only work on files under a certain size
head shows the last lines; tail the first
questions.explanation: Both default to 10 lines; -n changes the count. tail -f additionally follows a growing file.

options: Only the characters, ignoring newlines
The number of words in its input
The number of lines in its input
The number of files given as arguments
questions.explanation: wc counts lines (-l), words (-w) or bytes/characters (-c) of its input.

questions.text: mkdir -p projects/app/src does what that plain mkdir can't?
options: creates the whole path, parents included, without erroring if they exist
sets full permissions on the new directory
creates the new directory as a specially protected read-only system folder
prints the path it created for scripting
questions.explanation: -p makes every missing parent and is idempotent — the staple of setup scripts.

options: tar -czf archive.tar.gz
tar -xzf archive.tar.gz
gzip -r archive.tar.gz
unzip archive.tar.gz
questions.explanation: x = extract, z = gzip, f = file. (-czf is the opposite: create. unzip is for .zip files.)

options: the file contents for the text *.log
only the current working directory, ignoring every nested subfolder below it
the current directory and all subdirectories for files named like *.log
the whole filesystem starting from root
questions.explanation: find recurses from the given start point (here ".") matching by name; grep is the one that searches contents.

options: a compressed copy of the file in your home
a hard backup that survives deleting the original
an alias command named config.yml
a symbolic link — a pointer to the original file
questions.explanation: Symlinks reference a path; edits through either name touch the same file, and the link breaks if the target moves.

questions.text: There is no dedicated "rename" command in basic shell usage because…
options: mv old.txt new.txt does the job — moving within a directory is renaming
renaming requires editing the filesystem's inode table manually by hand
cp followed by rm is the only safe approach
files can only be renamed from a file manager
questions.explanation: A rename is just a move to a new name; mv handles both cases with one syntax.

options: display extended attributes of files
build and run commands from items arriving on stdin
parse command-line flags inside shell scripts
repeat the previously run command with new arguments
questions.explanation: Classic pattern: find . -name '*.tmp' | xargs rm — xargs turns the piped list into arguments for the next command.

questions.text: export VAR=value differs from plain VAR=value because export…
options: writes the variable out to a config file for persistence
makes the variable read-only in the current shell
passes the variable to child processes the shell starts
shares the variable with other logged-in users
questions.explanation: Un-exported variables live only in the current shell; exported ones enter the environment your scripts and commands inherit.

questions.text: SSH key-based login works because the server's ~/.ssh/authorized_keys file holds…
options: the login passwords allowed for that account, stored hashed
the private keys of every permitted user
a list of IP addresses allowed to connect
the public keys whose private halves may authenticate
questions.explanation: The client proves possession of the private key; the server checks it against the stored public key. Private keys never leave the client.

options: the live systemd journal for that service, following new lines
the complete unit file configuration currently used for that service
the journal for every unit except that one
a summary of the service's resource usage
questions.explanation: -u filters by unit, -f follows like tail -f — the first stop when a systemd service misbehaves.

questions.text: command | tee output.log does what a plain redirect doesn't?
options: appends to the end of the file instead of overwriting it
writes to the file and still shows the output on screen
strips colour codes before saving
compresses the output while writing it
questions.explanation: tee splits the stream: one copy to the file, one to stdout — so you watch and record simultaneously.

questions.text: strace is the tool you reach for when you need to…
options: profile precisely which functions in your own source code run the slowest
trace network packets between two hosts
see every system call a process makes (files opened, calls failing)
measure disk throughput under load
questions.explanation: strace reveals what a process really asks the kernel — gold for "why can't it find its config file" mysteries.

options: stores the boot-critical system binaries and therefore must never be touched
holds each user's process history logs
caches recently run programs for faster startup
is a virtual filesystem exposing live kernel and process state as files
questions.explanation: /proc/<pid>/... and files like /proc/meminfo are generated on read — tools like ps and top are largely /proc readers.

questions.text: "Too many open files" errors relate to…
options: file-descriptor limits (ulimit -n) that the process has exhausted
the disk running out of inodes
too many users editing the same file
the filesystem's write-ahead journals silently filling up over time
questions.explanation: Every socket and open file consumes a descriptor; leaks or high concurrency hit the per-process limit, raised via ulimit/systemd.

questions.text: set -euo pipefail at the top of a bash script means…
options: run in POSIX-compatibility mode with verbose errors
exit on any error or unset variable, and fail a pipeline if any stage fails
log every command executed to a pipe file
interactively prompt before every potentially destructive command gets executed
questions.explanation: -e stops on errors, -u catches typo'd variables, pipefail stops cmd|grep|awk from hiding a failing first stage — the safe-scripting trio.
